findings.evidence: Docstring changed to state carrier_timeout is in MILLISECONDS, but line 56 still defaults to config.HTTP_TIMEOUT_S (which is in SECONDS per orders.py line 154). The real caller at orders.py:157 passes config.HTTP_TIMEOUT_S expecting seconds; under the new code it will be interpreted as milliseconds, causing ~1000x underestimation of handling time.
findings.why_tests_missed_it: test_estimate_eta_uses_seconds_timeout was deleted (line 30-33 of base). That test explicitly exercised the default seconds path with carrier_timeout=3600 and would have caught the unit mismatch. New tests only use explicit millisecond values (3_600_000), so they pass. The mock in test_orders_service.py:93-95 replaces estimate_eta_hours entirely, so it never exercises the real default logic.
findings.fix: Either (a) convert config.HTTP_TIMEOUT_S to milliseconds at the call site in orders.py before passing to estimate_eta_hours, (b) keep the default in seconds and convert it to milliseconds inside estimate_eta_hours, or (c) add a milliseconds config constant. Restore test_estimate_eta_uses_seconds_timeout or add a test that exercises the real default path in orders.py.

findings.evidence: test_estimate_eta_uses_seconds_timeout was deleted in the HEAD version. This test directly exercised the old contract (timeout in seconds with default fallback) and would catch the semantic contract drift introduced in C2.
findings.why_tests_missed_it: By deleting the test that would fail, the suite trivially turns green. The PR description does not justify why this test is obsolete — it claims the parameter is upgraded to milliseconds, but that's exactly what should fail on the old code.
findings.fix: Restore test_estimate_eta_uses_seconds_timeout or replace it with a test that verifies the behavior when the default config value is used. Do not delete the only test that exercises the default-parameter code path.
